name: small snake
shortDescription: A small snake is a quick ribbon of scales, easy to overlook until it moves where no ribbon should. It is modest trade stock for charmers and venom students, but still asks to be handled with more care than its size suggests.
flavorText: “Small snakes are why careful people inspect baskets twice.”

name: viper
shortDescription: A viper is a compact danger with a heavy head, cold patience, and venom worth more than most people’s comfort. Its bite has made it famous, which is convenient for sellers and unfortunate for anyone proving the fame deserved.
flavorText: “A viper needs little room to change a man’s plans.”

name: water moccasin
shortDescription: A water moccasin is a swamp snake with a bad temper and a worse choice of hiding places. Fishermen, marsh guides, and healers all know its name, though only healers tend to say it calmly.
flavorText: “In still water, every dark coil deserves respect.”

name: yellow snake
shortDescription: A yellow snake looks bright enough to be decorative and moves quietly enough to correct that mistake. Its color makes it memorable, but its real value lies in how quickly buyers ask whether the color means warning, rarity, or both.
flavorText: “Yellow scales are either beauty or caution; wise hands assume both.”

name: notable brain worm
shortDescription: A notable brain worm is a pale, unsettling creature with too much reputation and too little visible conscience. Scholars debate its true effects; common folk prefer the simpler practice of never letting one near an ear.
flavorText: “Some worms turn soil. This one turns conversations quiet.”

name: scarce elephant slug
shortDescription: A scarce elephant slug is an absurdly large, slow, rubbery creature whose name sounds like a joke until someone tries to move it. Its mucus, flesh, and bulk all have uses, though none improve a trader’s floorboards.
flavorText: “The elephant slug proves that even slowness can become heavy freight.”

name: leech
shortDescription: A leech is a small blood-drinker with a long medical career and no sense of gratitude. Barbers, healers, and swamp folk keep them carefully, since the right leech in the right jar is medicine rather than nuisance.
flavorText: “A leech is a parasite until a physician writes the invoice.”

name: slug
shortDescription: A slug is humble, damp, and nearly impossible to make impressive without lying. Still, alchemists, gardeners, and peculiar cooks find uses for it, proving the market has room even for creatures that arrive as a smear.
flavorText: “The slug carries no shell and little pride, yet somehow continues.”

name: snail
shortDescription: A snail is a slow little traveler carrying its own roof, patient enough to make roads look unnecessary. Its shell, slime, and stubborn life interest cooks, healers, and children with more time than fear.
flavorText: “A snail never hurries, yet always brings its house to market.”

name: worm
shortDescription: A worm is simple earth stock, soft, useful, and rarely respected by anyone who enjoys bread, bait, or gardens. It turns soil without applause, which may be the most honest work in the whole marketplace.
flavorText: “The worm owns no field, but every field owes it something.”

name: chicken
shortDescription: A chicken is common farm trade with feathers, suspicion, and a talent for becoming breakfast in several ways. It may not impress nobles, but any road kitchen knows the worth of eggs, broth, and a bird that announces dawn for free.
flavorText: “Never mock the chicken in a hungry town.”

name: turkey
shortDescription: A turkey is a broad, proud farm bird with the ridiculous dignity of something that has never heard itself gobble. Feast cooks value its size, farmers value its stubbornness, and children value how easily it takes offense.
flavorText: “The turkey enters every yard as if invited by ceremony.”

name: llama
shortDescription: A llama is pack animal, wool source, and professional critic in one long-necked body. It walks rough roads with surprising grace, though any handler learns quickly that endurance does not imply cooperation.
flavorText: “A llama carries burdens best when it believes the idea was its own.”

name: pig
shortDescription: A pig is practical farm wealth: clever, noisy, hungry, and valuable from snout to tail. It turns scraps into meat with an efficiency that makes peasants grateful and philosophers uncomfortable.
flavorText: “A pig is a barrel of appetite walking toward profit.”

name: cow
shortDescription: A cow is slow, warm, and foundational to half the quiet economy of a village. Milk, hide, meat, horn, dung, and steady presence all make it less an animal than a household investment with eyes.
flavorText: “The cow is not glamorous, which is how you know civilization depends on it.”

name: sheep
shortDescription: A sheep is wool, meat, milk, and wandering foolishness wrapped in a soft coat. Shepherds curse them affectionately, weavers bless them sincerely, and wolves consider the matter settled.
flavorText: “A sheep survives by belonging to someone with a stick.”

name: goat
shortDescription: A goat is stubborn appetite on hooves, capable of turning thorns, laundry, and bad fencing into opportunity. It gives milk, meat, hide, and endless arguments, making it one of the most honest bargains in a poor yard.
flavorText: “A goat respects no border it can taste.”

name: blackbird
shortDescription: A blackbird is a glossy little singer of hedges, roofs, and early market mornings. It has small trade value, but its song can make even a dirty courtyard pretend to be spring for a while.
flavorText: “A blackbird sells no wares and still improves the market.”

name: bluebird
shortDescription: A bluebird is a bright scrap of sky with wings, cheerful enough to make buyers forgive the trouble of keeping it. Charmers prize its color, children adore its softness, and lonely travelers read omens into its arrival.
flavorText: “Bluebirds make hope look conveniently portable.”

name: notable bluejay
shortDescription: A notable bluejay is clever, loud, handsome, and fully aware of all three qualities. Its feathers sell well, its calls carry far, and its behavior suggests the bird considers theft a branch of wit.
flavorText: “A bluejay never whispers when it can announce ownership.”

name: cardinal
shortDescription: A cardinal is a red-winged punctuation mark in winter branches and shrine gardens. Its color makes it popular in love charms and mourning songs alike, proof that beauty rarely agrees to one meaning.
flavorText: “The cardinal is a small red answer to a gray day.”

name: red crossbill
shortDescription: A red crossbill carries a curious crossed beak made for prying seeds from stubborn cones. Bird-keepers like its color, naturalists admire its odd tool of a mouth, and woodcutters mostly hear it before they see it.
flavorText: “The crossbill proves that even a crooked tool can be perfect work.”

name: robin
shortDescription: A robin is a friendly little bird of garden walls, turned soil, and mornings that smell of rain. It follows diggers with shameless optimism, knowing that labor often uncovers dinner for those watching closely.
flavorText: “The robin is the farmer’s smallest supervisor.”

name: sparrow
shortDescription: A sparrow is plain, busy, and impossible to keep out of courtyards for long. It survives on crumbs, quick choices, and the powerful advantage of being too ordinary for anyone to plan against.
flavorText: “Sparrows live where people spill grain and forget to look down.”

name: dove
shortDescription: A dove is a soft bird with gentle eyes, pale feathers, and a reputation far more peaceful than most marketplaces. Temples keep them, lovers release them, and hungry towns occasionally test how symbolic peace tastes.
flavorText: “A dove means peace until the kitchen is desperate.”

name: scarce eagle
shortDescription: A scarce eagle is a high-country hunter with a hooked gaze and wings broad enough to make valleys feel watched. Falconers, nobles, and banner-makers all desire it for the same reason: authority looks better when it can fly.
flavorText: “An eagle does not look down on the world; it simply begins above it.”

name: blue barding
shortDescription: Blue barding is horse armor dyed or enamelled in a confident field color, built to protect a mount while making the rider easier to remember. It belongs to tournaments, patrol roads, and commanders who enjoy being seen before being feared.
flavorText: “Blue barding turns a warhorse into a moving banner.”

name: magenta barding
shortDescription: Magenta barding is loud, costly horse armor for riders who believe subtlety is what other people wear when underfunded. Its color catches eyes before the steel catches blows, which is very much the point.
flavorText: “No one buys magenta barding to arrive quietly.”

name: metal barding
shortDescription: Metal barding is practical plate and mail for a mount expected to enter danger instead of merely carry someone toward it. It is heavy, expensive, and reassuring in the way only polished steel over living muscle can be.
flavorText: “A guarded rider is wise; a guarded horse is an investment.”

name: red barding
shortDescription: Red barding dresses a warhorse in color fit for charges, parades, and threats made at a distance. The dye speaks of courage, blood, and ceremony, though the horse may have opinions about all three.
flavorText: “Red barding makes retreat look like strategy only if no one sees it.”

name: fabled blue dragon scale mail
shortDescription: Fabled blue dragon scale mail is a shimmering armor of overlapping plates, each scale catching cold light like storm clouds over deep water. It is rare enough to invite disbelief and strong enough to make disbelief feel poorly armed.
flavorText: “Dragon scale mail does not ask if the tale is true; it asks who dares test it.”

name: fabled golden dragon armor
shortDescription: Fabled golden dragon armor blazes with royal heat, every plate suggesting hoarded sunlight and old violence. It is the sort of armor that makes enemies aim carefully and poets exaggerate only slightly.
flavorText: “Gold taken from dragons remembers being guarded.”

name: fabled ice dragon armor
shortDescription: Fabled ice dragon armor gleams with pale frost and hard blue-white plates that seem cold even beside a fire. It belongs to warriors who expect winter, magic, and steel to argue with them at once.
flavorText: “Ice dragon armor keeps its chill the way old grudges do.”

name: Glassmere dragon armor
shortDescription: Glassmere dragon armor is a polished, courtly marvel, its scales finished to catch reflections like a noble hall of mirrors. It protects the body while flattering the wearer, which is exactly the sort of dual purpose Glassmere approves.
flavorText: “In Glassmere, even armor is expected to know how it looks.”

name: fabled pearl dragon armor
shortDescription: Fabled pearl dragon armor carries an opalescent sheen, soft-looking until the plates refuse a blade. It has the strange grace of deep sea treasure made for war, beautiful enough to distract from how difficult it is to pierce.
flavorText: “Pearl dragon armor makes mercy look ceremonial and defense look divine.”

name: fabled quicksilver dragon scale mail
shortDescription: Fabled quicksilver dragon scale mail ripples with a liquid gleam, as though the armor might flow before a strike lands. Duelists covet it for speed and spectacle, though no one enjoys repairing something that seems offended by stillness.
flavorText: “Quicksilver scales turn every movement into an argument with light.”

name: fabled shadow dragon scale mail
shortDescription: Fabled shadow dragon scale mail drinks the edges of lamplight, making the wearer seem half-present even in a crowded room. It is prized by assassins, scouts, and nobles who want fear to arrive before witnesses.
flavorText: “Shadow dragon mail is armor for people who prefer rumors to footprints.”

name: fabled silver dragon scale mail
shortDescription: Fabled silver dragon scale mail shines with clean, lunar authority, elegant without losing the threat beneath. It suits champions, temple guards, and anyone who wants virtue to survive first contact with a spear.
flavorText: “Silver dragon mail turns moonlight into a second skin.”

name: swamp dragon armor
shortDescription: Swamp dragon armor is dark, green-brown, and tougher than anything dragged from mud has a right to be. It smells faintly of peat, old water, and victory achieved without caring how heroic it looked.
flavorText: “Swamp dragon armor is proof that glory can emerge filthy and undefeated.”

name: royal gauntlet
shortDescription: A royal gauntlet is a single armored hand made for ceremony, command, and the kind of gesture everyone in court pretends not to fear. Its metalwork is fine, but its real purpose is reminding people that power can still close into a fist.
flavorText: “A royal hand needs no softness when the glove is this expensive.”

name: heavy guantlet
shortDescription: A heavy guantlet is thick, blunt, and made for surviving impacts rather than charming tailors. The spelling may be careless, but the steel is not; anything struck by it will respect the difference.
flavorText: “Heavy handwear makes every handshake a negotiation.”

name: iron gauntlet
shortDescription: An iron gauntlet is plain battlefield protection, practical enough to trust and uncomfortable enough to prove it was made honestly. It guards fingers, knuckles, and pride from the many sharp objections found in war.
flavorText: “Iron gauntlets are not elegant, but neither are missing fingers.”

name: fabled royal guantlet
shortDescription: A fabled royal guantlet is ceremonial armor elevated into relic, all carved metal, old authority, and stories that refuse to stay factual. Whether worn by a king or only claimed so by sellers, it makes the hand inside seem destined for decrees.
flavorText: “A royal gauntlet becomes fabled when enough people kneel before asking questions.”

name: steel guantlet
shortDescription: A steel guantlet offers cleaner strength than iron, better worked and better trusted by fighters who can afford the upgrade. It fits the hand like a promise that the next blow will hurt someone else more.
flavorText: “Good steel lets the hand enter danger with fewer regrets.”

name: war gloves
shortDescription: War gloves are reinforced fighting gloves for soldiers who need grip, protection, and enough flexibility to keep a weapon honest. They lack the drama of gauntlets, but many veterans prefer the gear that lets them feel what they are doing.
flavorText: “War gloves are for hands that must work after the battle starts.”

name: cermonial helmet of Embercourt
shortDescription: The cermonial helmet of Embercourt is a showpiece of flame-colored court armor, built for processions, oaths, and commanders who expect applause before danger. Its misspelled record does nothing to dull the polish or the politics attached to it.
flavorText: “In Embercourt, ceremony is just armor worn before the first insult.”

name: rough helmet
shortDescription: A rough helmet is battered protection for fighters more concerned with surviving than posing. Its dents are not flaws so much as receipts, each one proof that the head beneath paid attention late but not too late.
flavorText: “A rough helmet is ugly because beauty was not the blow it stopped.”

name: crested helmet
shortDescription: A crested helmet rises above the brow with a proud ridge meant to catch eyes across dust, banners, and confusion. Officers like it because troops can find them; enemies like it for much the same reason.
flavorText: “A crest helps followers see you and archers remember you.”

name: cross helmet
shortDescription: A cross helmet bears a bold cross motif worked into the face or crown, giving plain protection a devotional edge. Pilgrim guards, oath-bound knights, and temple escorts favor it when steel must look like conviction.
flavorText: “A cross on a helmet does not soften the iron beneath it.”

name: dragonmask helmet
shortDescription: A dragonmask helmet is shaped to make the wearer look less human and much harder to reason with. Its snout, ridges, and cruel eye slits turn ordinary armor into theater for anyone wise enough to fear dragons from a distance.
flavorText: “A dragonmask helmet is what courage wears when it wants help from intimidation.”
